methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Quilter is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company founded by Sergiy Nesterenko that has raised $15M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a sector universe of 9 private peers (including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity) and 1 public comparable (277810.KQ), though a sufficiently tight peer cohort at matching stage and valuation tier was not available for reliable median benchmarking.

Stage-progression analysis proves the primary applicable method. Using the $15M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a standard 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement to subsequent rounds, the method yields $30M. This reflects the expected value accretion between early and mid-stage financing rounds in capital-intensive sectors like robotics.

The peer-median approach was not feasible due to insufficient datapoints within narrow cohorts (zero same-stage peers, only two adjacent-stage comparables identified, zero similar-tier matches). Relying on the broader seven-company sector universe would introduce unacceptable precision loss given the wide valuation dispersion typical in robotics at varying stages.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $26M to $34M, with a median estimate of $30M. Key uncertainty remains the company's unit economics and market traction relative to the identified peer set—stage-progression alone cannot account for business quality variance. Validation against subsequent funding round outcomes and peer performance will be essential.
